speaker: interviewee
text: --in Mexico and [INTERPOSING VOICES] developed pneumonia and infection. And so that happened to my aunt too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if he could have the best--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Jewish? Both were, weren't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were not, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- according to the Jewish law, they were not Jewish, because the mother was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but the father was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The father was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. The father--

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their father was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah the father was.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Gabor's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's July 7, 1995. We're interviewing Magda Mautner Marx-- M-A-G-D-A, M-A-U-T-N-E-R, M-A-R-X, whose birth name was Klein-- K-L-E-I-N. The interviewer, myself, is Rose Sher Weiss-- R-O-S-E, S-H-E-R, W-E-I-S-S, in Pembroke Pines, Broward County, Florida, USA, in the English language. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Magda Mautner Marx, nee Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-G-D-A, M-A-U-T-N-E-R, M-A-R-X, nee K-L-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have a Hebrew name-- Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This in Hebrew-- M-A-L-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date-- date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up in Budapest, Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what schools you attended-- not in detail, just generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended eight years in the elementary and higher elementary school in Budapest, in the Kazinczy Utcai Orthodox Jewish Congregation school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- one year I went to the Jewish gymnasium. And from there, I had four years in high-- high school, commercial high school, that I graduated. And then when I was already married, I went to a technological, I say, college, or [NON-ENGLISH] they call it, that I learned my profession. Rather, this was my husband profession, but I learned to work with him. And I got four diplomas in my field.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your field?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm an orthopedic technician-- brace maker, orthopedic corset maker, hernia maker, and corset maker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us how your family supported itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a business in-- then. We buy the store big-- for-- for winemaking and winemaking equipment. And then later, he sold this business and opened a bank. And then he sold the bank. At that time, there were a big inflation in Germany. And he went to Berlin and bought, I think, three apartment buildings. Later, he sold that, and he got an offer to buy some oil wells in Drohobych. This is between Romania or-- or Poland. I am not so sure the part. And he put all his money in this business. And then the-- were-- were partners-- they opened a big office in Vienna. And then the partners one day disappeared. And my father left without a penny. And even he had debts.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had to sell my mother's jewelry and silver, and everything what was available. And so this are how I grew up. Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they affiliated with a shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. With the orthodox Kazinczy Street shul

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years back does your family consider itself living in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back hundreds and hundreds of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any political interests of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. He was in the Mizrachi Movement. Otherwise, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you? Did you have any political interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only in the Zionist organization, also from my early childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware, living in Budapest, of what was happening to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was much, much later. Germany was a very democratic country at that time. In Hungary, I was aware of it, because in Hungary, there were so many political changes. And there were, I think, they were born anti-Semitic. In 19-- after the first world war, where my father was enrolled in the war, as I-- I don't know what he did. I think he did office work in the war. And after the war finished, in 1918, it became somehow the communist took over. And it was a terrible time-- a terrible time, where there was no food. My-- luckily, my father was-- we were a lot of children-- he was able to provide us with-- with food. But then the communist were conquered [BACKGROUND NOISE] by Horthy. And then came the so-called White Terror. And then all my relatives from Hungary, from the country, they came up to Budapest. Because they thought in a big city, it's easier to escape somehow. And I remember that I-- one of my cousins came home one day, and he was all blue and black. And his fingers were all bloody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were captured by [NON-ENGLISH]. They were the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the people who were-- who-- students in the university. They-- they captured him on the street, and they beat him up. And I remember that he told that they had some kind of stick with-- with the nails in it. And they put in into his finger to take out the whole blood. And then they asked, what happened to you, young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was so smart. He said, I fell, and I hit myself. Otherwise, they will-- wouldn't let him even go. So and-- and there were terrible, terrible things. Everybody knows who is Hungarian what happened in Orgovany, for instance, and some other towns-- that they just killed the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was that, Magda?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1919 and 1920, maybe, also. And then it was-- Horthy became the governor of Hungary, and then somehow, a little consolidated the situation. But he brought laws against the Jews also. Because that time, they made the numerous clauses. Did you hear about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were not-- only was permitted 6% of the universities to be Jewish student. So, all my cousins and relatives, they went to Italy, and Germany, and other countries to-- to college. So they are my early memories of anti-Semitism. In the-- Did you personally experience anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally experienced. I think this is very-- very important later in my life, because I learned something out of it. And this was when I went to this commercial high school. We were only four Jewish girl in this class, because I went some far away, where was not a Jewish neighborhood. And my parents didn't permit that I travel. So I had to walk about an hour from my home to this school. And it was a very, very cold day. In Hungary, it can be very cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I arrived. And somehow, I was the first in the class, and I was frozen. So I hid behind the-- a stove. There were a stove-- how they heated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, came-- opened the door-- and came in three girls. And they had these wooden pencil holder that they used that. And they started to hit the-- bang and they sing-- 'If you want to have Hungary happy, kill all the Jews mercilessly.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I were-- they didn't see me, because I was behind the-- the stove. So for-- for a second, I didn't know what-- what to do. What should I say? They know that I am Jewish. And we were good friends. So I came out. In one second, I decided what I have to do. I came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they were stunned that I was there. And then I step up to the platform where the-- where the teacher should be. And I told them, now sit down. They were stunned. They were sitting down. And then I stood there, and I told them-- 'Shame on you what you said.'

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Now you have to know who I am. If it will be still an-- a Jewish country, I will be a princess. Because I have proof that I am a descendant of King David. And who are you?' So they were-- they didn't say a word. And there came other-- a girls. But I think this was the minute-- then I learned that I have to be somehow above the-- above the others to feel my personality, and to feel secure. And this what I followed, and made me a survivor. I think this was the turning point. And what happened to your family after your father lost his money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my brother, with some relatives, went to Palestine. I don't know how they do-- did that, because it was the English rule there. And they didn't allow let Jewish to immigrate. Some going in a black way. And then he wrote to my father. Now you have to start from scratch. Don't do it in Hungary. Because that time was already the 30-- 30th. And my father heard enough about starting the Nazism. And my brother was smart enough to know that this will spread to Hungary also. So my father went to Palestine. And he managed, somehow, to get an affidavit from some relatives who-- who prove that he has, I think, the 50,000 pounds, or pounds sterling. I don't know what was the money at that time. And so he could go officially to Palestine. And then a year later, they brought the whole family-- my mother and the eight children. One brother was dead. And I was already married at that time. This was in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay behind--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because of your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my husband had old parents there. And we started already a little business. So he didn't want to go. And he paid with his life for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did your family go to Palestine-- all of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year later. I think in '36 or '37-- just the last minute. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your life like in Hungary with your husband, just before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war we had a nice life. We had a rowboat. He was a sportsman. And we went to tennis, and hiking, and skiing. So we had-- but it was very short, unfortunately-- very short, happy life. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: These thousand started there. They started in 1938, when was the-- was the Austrian onslaught. when the German moved in Austria. Austria and Hungary is very close geographically-- only geographically. And just then I became pregnant. But we decided, no. We don't want to stay in Hungary. I have to have an abortion. And we were about 20 young couples. All-- all of us had skills-- lawyers. technicians, opticians-- all kinds. So everybody had something. You know, they would never be a burden on any country. And we decided that, as a group, we want to leave Hungary. And we gave everywhere-- I think is no country in the world where we didn't apply to permit to move there. And everywhere we were, we were refused, including the United States, Australia, New Zealand, Canada-- whatever I can think of it. So-- and in 1939, they took already the young men. They come-- 1939 came the second law against the Jews. And they took in labor camps then in Hungary. And in 1940, also my husband was summoned also. And 1941 again, they were in Nagykata-- is the name of this town where they have to go to the-- they was not the army, because they didn't have that men or uniform-- nothing. So-- and this was it. And then come 1942. And then my husband and-- and other, I think-- I think about 60,000 labor-- in the labor camp, took to Ukraine. And I never heard about him. I got one-- one little-- I'll show it to you-- a little piece of paper. It-- it came to me as soldier and ask for a small amount of money. And that was it. And in 19-- what about this year-- it was 19-- 1943, I got the official notice that he's missing in action. But I know they all died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Ukraine. This was cruel, more cruel than any concentration camp-- what they did with these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what kind of work these 60,000 men did in the Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. There they had to take out mines, and they had to build the railroads. Did-- can you imagine? This was about two meters snow in 1942, there in the Ukraine, and it was 42 minus Celsius degree. If somebody was sitting down, never got up. And this was not even the German were so cruel. The Hungarian were so cruel. They-- I am sure I am not to tell, because this is hearsay. I was not there. But it was terrible what they did. There were, for instant, was there one man, Attila Petschauer, who had seven gold medal in Olympics as-- in fencing-- in fencing. He was Jewish. And after the war, we heard that they knew who he was. And because of that, they were so cruel to him, that they hit him so that when they found after the war-- somebody found him-- there was no-- it was no skin and was no-- the whole spine was open-- they killed him-- terribly cruel things they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose name?

speaker: interviewer
text: This gentleman that you're describing now, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Attila Petschauer. He was a very famous-- very, very famous. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it ever substantiated how your husband died in the Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But it could have been two ways. This was when Voronezh-- this was before Stalingrad. And the Russian advanced very, very fast. So they captured a lot of people. But they couldn't care for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they captured and let them go. And there was no food, no, no shelter, no nothing. So a lot of them just got frozen-- and the epidemic. So some there, they died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not one of his group came back. But before they took them to Ukraine, they had the-- I don't know how you call it-- not a general-- a little lower than a general-- who was the leader of these groups. At that time, they took four squadrons together, and he gave a speech. 'You lousy Jews have to know that not one of them will come back alive.' This man was hang after the war-- Murai. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- this was the story. And I was, of course, alone with-- with the other women. So we formed kind of a chain, that if somebody has some news, call the others. So-- because one-- one news give hope to the others. And I was the lucky one that I got always three, four times news about my husband. After that, he-- I got already that he disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: But believe that somehow one was-- and this I have to tell, because nobody could believe it. And I can never believe it. One Sunday, we had already the yellow star, and the building had the yellow star. One morning, a Sunday morning, came a man. He was dressed like a-- like formal, for Sunday-- boots, and a nice coat, and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the super let him in, because it was not a Jewish man-- let him in. And he came and he said-- he rung the bell, and I-- I had my son on my arm. And I opened the door. And he say, are you Mrs. Mautner? I said, yes. May I come in? Yes, come in. So I brought some news about your husband. I thought that he-- I started to shake, you know? And I invited him come in. Sit down. And he told me the following story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was in Ukraine. And he managed to-- to take 10 men somehow to Turkey. And he gave me your address. And he has a photograph and a letter for you-- to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, do you have the letter? No, no, no. He had to sew into his coat. And now he's in a quarantine. And he cannot take it out, because it's forbidden there. He doesn't want that anybody knows about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and-- and he said-- I said, this is beautiful of you that you came. And he started to cry. The tears were running on his face.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am a father, also. And I understand how you feel. And this is my duty, my Christian duty, to bring you this news. So I ask him, can I help you with something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. I-- may I pay you back your-- I don't know. He came from somewhere-- the country No, no, no, no, no. Nothing, nothing, nothing. And he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, of course, I called all the women that good news. We don't know who the 10 men is, but everybody can have some hope out of it. So-- and I asked this man to give the name of the quarantine and his-- his son, and his name. So I right away I wrote to this quarantine

speaker: interviewee
text: And about a week later, I got back my letter. Such quarantine doesn't exist in Hungary. [BACKGROUND NOISE] You-- you have some mistake. I got back the letter. So I wrote to this man. I had his address was Yanil Kovatch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Kovatch-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a place in Hungary. So I wrote there. And I wrote Mr. Kovatch, I don't know if I wrote down or you had some mistake, because I got back this letter that this quarantine doesn't exist. And would you be so kind and to write me again down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So again a week later I got a letter. It was a business letter from the same name what this man gave me. And he wrote, dear madam, you must-- this must have been a [? hoax ?] or somebody-- I-- I don't understand what happened. But, I saw what it is all about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have no children. I have nobody in the army. And even I saw what is the situation. I went around in the whole town and asked if somebody knows something about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- until now, I don't know why this man came, why he told me this story, why he was crying. He didn't want to have money. I don't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got two-- two more news this way about my husband. And after the war, I found out that all was a lie. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Cut.
